profile.name: Jesudas Antonyraj
profile.tagline: Digital Transformation | E-Commerce & Cloud — 19+ years scaling engineering orgs at Tesco, Condé Nast, Ascena, and Target.
profile.bio: Engineering Director with 19+ years of experience building and scaling e-commerce technology platforms across Tesco, Ascena Retail Group, Condé Nast, and Target Corporation. Track record delivering enterprise digital transformation programs: architected golden-source data platforms that drove 20% revenue growth, led a Global Capability Center workforce migration with zero productivity loss, and reduced security incidents by 90%. Executive MBA from Olin Business School, Washington University in St. Louis, and IIT Bombay, combining business strategy with engineering leadership. Outside of the day job, I build AI-powered tools and full-stack side projects — from resume screening to survey generation to subscription tracking.

education.degree: Executive MBA — Leadership, Digital Transformation and Cost Management
education.school: Olin Business School, Washington University in St. Louis & IIT Bombay

bullets: Own the engineering roadmap for Tesco's e-commerce data platform; delivered the "One Data, One Source" golden-source architecture across 8+ product lines, improving operational efficiency by 30% and contributing to a 20% increase in topline revenue.

Founded and led a Center of Excellence combining DevOps automation, observability (New Relic, Datadog, Splunk), and security governance, reducing security incidents by 90% and cutting third-party vendor costs by 10% within 18 months.

Built and retained a high-performance engineering organization of cross-functional squads; introduced structured career frameworks and data-driven 1:1s that improved retention by 65% and raised stakeholder NPS to 85%.
Led cloud-native migration and CI/CD maturity initiatives on AWS and GCP, enabling weekly release cadence in place of quarterly release trains and reducing deployment risk by more than half.
Optimized distributed systems architecture using microservices patterns and performance monitoring tools, increasing platform throughput by 25% for high-traffic digital publishing properties.
Directed an ISO 27001-aligned compliance and security upgrade program covering encryption, automated testing, and access governance; achieved zero audit findings on first external assessment.
Led front-end engineering for 8 enterprise retail websites; standardized a React-based component library that cut development effort by 45% and reduced average delivery cycle from 6 weeks to 5 days.

Led migration of 70% of the engineering workforce from South America to India during a business crisis, achieving zero productivity loss, zero client escalations, and significant annualized cost reduction.
Conceived and delivered a hackathon-winning personalized coupon engine (React, RESTful APIs) that generated a 30% revenue uplift and was fast-tracked to production within 60 days.
Reduced operational costs by 30% and improved team retention by 30% through Scrum maturity initiatives, workforce planning tools, and structured performance review cycles.
Accelerated feature delivery by 20% and reduced release cycles by 30% by introducing Agile project governance (Jira, Confluence) and continuous integration practices across 4 engineering squads.

articles.title: The Platform Gap: What China Built That We Didn't
articles.excerpt: China built platforms, chips, and industrial ecosystems. India built a services economy. A look at what changed — and what it would take to build our own tech stack.

body.text: India chose the services route over manufacturing. China focused on core industries, R&D, and platform creation, while India became a global service provider — cheap labour, IT back-office, and customer support.
Dependency vs. Self-Reliance
We import chips, hardware, and tools, then write code on top of them. Meanwhile, China builds the chips, the tools, the infrastructure, and the software.

India's policies often favour quick results — outsourcing, foreign investment in services — rather than nurturing long-term tech ecosystems, with little strategic intent to build globally competitive platforms from scratch.
What Needs to Change

items: Shift focus from services to a product/platform economy
Incentivise deep tech, chip design, robotics, and industrial automation
Government-backed moonshot projects in core sectors
Encourage youth to build, not just code
Embed first-principles thinking and engineering rigour in education

body.text: Final Thought

We missed the bus, but India doesn't need to catch up — we need to build a better road. We have the brains, the market, the culture, and the drive. What's missing is vision and political-economic will.

articles.title: How Product Engineering Is Changing the Future of Tech
articles.excerpt: The future leans more toward product engineering than traditional software engineering — coding becomes a commodity; solving real problems and execution become the differentiators.

body.text: The future leans more towards product engineering than traditional software engineering, where coding knowledge becomes a commodity, and products solving real problems and execution are the true differentiators in product development.

articles.title: How AI Can Make Software Engineering More Interesting
articles.excerpt: AI isn't coming for engineers' jobs — it's coming for the boring parts. Embracing it means more time for the problems that actually matter.

body.text: Engineers + AI: Skip the Boring Parts.

Let's be real about AI and coding — it's not coming for our jobs, it's coming for the boring parts. We engineers waste over half our time writing repetitive, commodity code that could easily be automated. AI is stepping in to handle that grunt work.

This isn't about replacement — it's about elevation. We'll finally have time to focus on the stuff that actually matters: solving interesting problems, designing better systems, and creating genuine value.
Embrace the AI revolution. It's not the end of software engineering — it's the beginning of software engineering that doesn't make you want to bang your head against your keyboard.

articles.title: Strategic Technology Investment: The Build vs Buy, vs Hybrid Dilemma
articles.excerpt: Large IT projects run 45% over budget and deliver 56% less value than predicted. A framework for deciding when to build, buy, or blend — and why leadership churn quietly wrecks more roadmaps than bad technology choices.

body.text: Many enterprises are caught in a costly cycle of building products that fail to deliver expected value. The data is stark: large IT projects run 45% over budget and deliver 56% less value than predicted. Only 29% of in-house development projects finish on time and budget, and 19% fail outright.
Leadership Transitions: The Hidden Cost Driver

Nearly 60% of new executives make substantial changes to existing projects within their first six months, regardless of performance — a pattern that costs Fortune 500 companies an estimated $1.5 billion annually in abandoned work.
Build
Best when a capability is a genuine competitive advantage or core IP. Companies that focus engineering effort on differentiating work see 3x higher engineering retention.
Buy

Best for standard, commodity functions. Organizations moving non-core functions to SaaS cut total cost of ownership by 40% over four years; mature vendor management correlates with 23% higher profit margins.
Hybrid

Combines both with clear boundaries — completes initiatives 30% faster and realizes ROI 25% sooner, but integration alone can consume 30-40% of project budgets, and five-year costs are typically underestimated by 40-60%.
The Decision Framework

items: Does this capability create competitive differentiation?
How deeply must it integrate with other systems?
Will building it create valuable knowledge and engage the team?
Do we have the sustained commitment to build and maintain it?

body.text: Organizational alignment breaks down at three junctures: when business defines value without understanding feasibility, when technology pursues perfection without confirming business impact, and when leadership changes direction without respecting institutional knowledge. Organizations allocating more than 55% of their IT budget to differentiating capabilities saw 2.8x higher revenue growth over five years.
